meta.format: The 2026 FIFA World Cup, co-hosted by the United States, Mexico and Canada from June 11 to July 19, 2026, features 48 teams split into 12 groups of four (A-L) playing a single round-robin group stage. The 12 group winners, 12 runners-up and the 8 best third-placed teams (32 teams total) advance to a new Round of 32, followed by a Round of 16, quarter-finals, semi-finals, a third-place play-off and the final, for 104 matches in all across 16 stadiums. Knockout ties go to 30 minutes of extra time and then penalties if level. The final is at MetLife Stadium, East Rutherford (New York/New Jersey) on July 19, 2026.
meta.thirdPlaceRule: The 12 third-placed teams are ranked by points, goal difference, goals scored, fair play points, then lots; the top 8 advance. Their assignment to specific Round-of-32 matches depends on which groups the 8 best thirds come from, per the official allocation table (Annex C of the FIFA regulations).

champion.reading: The orb glows crimson and gold: a side unbeaten in thirty competitive battles does not stumble in a summer built for those who keep the ball away from the sun. Every model, every simulation, every line of the ledger points to the same midfield cathedral — and the boy Yamal, returning from the hamstring's betrayal, saves his fury for the knockout rounds. Spain lift it in New Jersey on July 19, their second star, the dynasty confirmed.

runnerUp.reading: The champions of Lusail march again — Messi, 39 in his record sixth World Cup, dragging destiny by the collar through Uruguay, Türkiye, Ronaldo's Portugal and English iron. But in the New Jersey twilight the dynasty of Yamal and Pedri proves one midfield too far. The old king scores in the final and leaves with silver — and with every heart in the stadium.

goldenBoot.reading: The orb shows nine goals and a bronze medal — the captain dragging France through the heat with ice from the spot, then two more in the third-place match to settle the count. Back-to-back Golden Boots, a feat for the ages, yet the trophy itself slips away a second time, this round to Spain's midfield cathedral. Kane goes deep but the penalties dry up; Haaland burns bright and brief.

oracle.boldPrediction: The orb stakes its reputation on this: Haaland's Norway will fell five-time champions Brazil in the round of 16 at MetLife on July 5 — the north wind ending the Seleção's summer at the earliest stage since 1990 — and the Río de la Plata clásico, Argentina against Bielsa's Uruguay, will detonate in the round of 32 as the match of the tournament. Believe neither bookmaker nor pundit: believe the wind.

championOdds.note: Opta 6.6%; bookmakers shorter at 8/1 on brand strength
Opta 7.0%; 23.9% semifinal rate in Opta sims
Opta 3.5% — 37 qualifying goals, unbeaten ahead of Italy
Opta 3.6% — drifting after De Ligt, Simons and Timber ruled out
Opta 2.1% — Copa 2024 runners-up, Luis Díaz in elite form
Opta 1.6% — golden generation past its peak
Opta 1.2% — best of the three co-hosts

darkHorses.why: Back after 28 years and arguably Europe's most in-form side — unbeaten through a qualifying group containing Italy with 37 goals. Haaland and Ødegaard headline; Opta rates them nearly level with the Netherlands. Models love them far more than the public — nobody wants them in the round of 16.

The 2022 semifinalists return deeper: Hakimi at his peak, Brahim Díaz and El Khannouss adding creativity to the famous defensive block. Continental-level support across US host cities, and a low-block-plus-transition identity built for tournament heat and knockout chaos.

Copa América 2024 runners-up with Luis Díaz one of the form players in world football. A suffocating midfield press and genuine defensive solidity — they can realistically win Group K outright over a heat-stressed Portugal, earning a soft bracket path.

A golden generation led by Arda Güler and Çalhanoğlu, fearless knockout pedigree from Euro 2024, and the kind of chaotic attacking ceiling that breaks brackets. The 48-team format's softer round of 32 gives streaky sides an extra runway.

narratives: The Last Dance squared: Messi (39 during the tournament) and Ronaldo (41) both make a record sixth World Cup — the final chapter of football's greatest rivalry.
Argentina chase the first back-to-back titles since Brazil 1962, while Spain attempt to add a World Cup to Euro 2024 and confirm a dynasty built around Yamal and Pedri.

Heat is the invisible 49th team: over a third of the 104 matches are projected at high-risk heat levels. Dallas and Houston are the worst offenders — Portugal opens with two Houston furnace games — and FIFA has mandated hydration breaks in every half.

The expanded 48-team, 104-match format across three countries means continental travel fatigue, a new round of 32 that protects giants but gives streaky dark horses a runway, and best-third-place math that will produce late group-stage chaos.

Host nation hopes diverge: Mexico opened with a feisty 2-0 win at the Azteca (three red cards — a World Cup opener record), while Opta gives the USMNT just 1.2% and Canada sweat on Alphonso Davies' fitness.

Deschamps' swansong: France's manager leaves after the tournament chasing Pozzo's 88-year-old record, while Tuchel attempts to end England's 60 years of hurt and Ancelotti tries to fix Brazil's 24-year drought.

An injury plague reshaped contenders before a ball was kicked: Rodrygo, Gnabry, Simons, De Ligt, Timber, Ekitiké and Fermín López are all out — and Yamal's hamstring is the single biggest fitness storyline.

contenders.dossier: Reigning European champions under Luis de la Fuente, unbeaten in roughly 30 competitive matches since March 2023, and the only team Opta rates more likely than not to reach the quarterfinals (52.1%). Their positional-play identity runs through the world's best midfield (Pedri, with Rodri anchoring) and the wing threat of Lamine Yamal and Nico Williams. The asterisks: Yamal missed the final month of the club season with a torn hamstring, while Fermín López is out with a fractured metatarsal. This is the most complete squad in the field.

Didier Deschamps' swansong after finals in 2018 (won) and 2022 (lost); he chases Pozzo's feat of two World Cups as coach. Mbappé captains a squad with Dembélé, Tchouaméni, Camavinga and Zaïre-Émery; France's tournament-mode pragmatism remains the best in the business. Ekitiké's Achilles tear trims striker depth, and they share a group with dangerous Norway.

Thomas Tuchel was hired to end 60 years of hurt, bringing German tournament pragmatism to a golden squad: Kane after a monster Bayern season, Bellingham, Saka, Foden and a deep young defense. They arrive without major injury crises — a rarity among contenders. Back-to-back Euro finals suggest the ceiling; the drought since 1966 is the weight.

Defending champions under Scaloni attempting the first repeat since Brazil 1962, built on the most settled core in the tournament — Martínez, Romero–Otamendi, De Paul, Mac Allister, Julián Álvarez. Messi, 39 during the tournament in a record sixth World Cup, returned from injury to score in the final warm-up. The machine no longer depends on him for 90 minutes — which is what makes them dangerous.

Roberto Martínez fields arguably the world's best midfield trio — Vitinha, João Neves, a career-peak Bruno Fernandes — with Leão and Félix in support and Ronaldo, 41, in a reduced talisman role. The looming problem: both opening games are in Houston, among the tournament's highest heat-risk fixtures — brutal for an ageing icon.

Ancelotti's first international job and one of the least-fancied Brazil squads ever (Opta 6.6%). Raphinha leads the attack with Vinícius in a form dip; Rodrygo is out with a torn ACL. The wild card is Neymar, 34, chasing the Ronaldo-2002 redemption arc. Five stars and a knockout whisperer — nobody wants them in a bracket.

Nagelsmann blends a Bayern-heavy spine — Neuer, Kimmich — with Musiala back from injury and Wirtz as the creative axis. June has been unkind: Gnabry tore an adductor and is out. Four titles say never count them out; group exits in 2018 and 2022 hang over them.

Koeman's side was Opta's top-rated outsider, but June 2026 gutted them: Xavi Simons (ACL), De Ligt (back surgery) and Timber (groin) all out. The remaining XI is top-eight quality but depth is a genuine liability over an eight-game run in heat. Three-time finalists who have never won it.

Mexico.name: Mexico
Mexico.style: Pragmatic 4-3-3 with a compact mid-block, direct pacey wingers and strong set pieces
keyPlayers: Raúl Jiménez (Fulham, ST — scored in the opener)
Mexico.form: Unbeaten through 2026 and reigning Gold Cup/Nations League champions; opened the World Cup with a 2-0 win over South Africa at the Azteca.
Mexico.outlook: Altitude, home noise and Jiménez's form make them group favorites (Opta 48%), but creativity vs low blocks is thin and César Montes is suspended for the South Korea game.

South Korea.name: South Korea
South Korea.style: Flexible 4-2-3-1/3-4-3 built around Kim Min-jae, organized mid-block with rapid vertical transitions
keyPlayers: Son Heung-min (LAFC, FW, captain — 9 goals in 15 MLS games in 2026)
Lee Kang-in (PSG, AM — assisted the equalizer vs Czechia)
Hwang In-beom (Feyenoord, CM — goal and assist vs Czechia)
South Korea.form: Only unbeaten AFC qualifier (6W 4D); came from behind to beat the Czech Republic 2-1 on matchday one.
South Korea.outlook: Elite spine and proven resilience, but heavy reliance on Kim Min-jae's fitness and Son's 33-year-old legs, with finishing efficiency a nagging worry.

Czech Republic.name: Czech Republic
Czech Republic.style: Pragmatic, physical low-block 3-4-2-1; the best set-piece team in UEFA qualifying, weaponizing Coufal's long throws
keyPlayers: Patrik Schick (Bayer Leverkusen, ST — 9 goals in his last 8 club games)
Ladislav Krejčí (Wolves, CB — scored vs Korea)
Czech Republic.form: Reached a first World Cup since 2006 via playoff shootout wins over Ireland and Denmark; won both June friendlies before the 2-1 loss to South Korea.
Czech Republic.outlook: Schick's red-hot form and dead-ball menace give a real third-place-or-better path, but open-play creativity is thin and the back line lacks pace.

South Africa.name: South Africa
South Africa.style: Disciplined 4-3-3 low-mid block with quick counters, built on a cohesive Mamelodi Sundowns core
keyPlayers: Teboho Mokoena (Mamelodi Sundowns, CM, set-piece specialist)
South Africa.form: Won CAF Group C, but exited AFCON in the last 16, drew Nicaragua 0-0, then lost 2-0 to Mexico with two red cards.
South Africa.outlook: Organized, cohesive and well-goalkept, but the attack is blunt and the suspensions of Sithole and Zwane gut them for the must-win Czechia game.

Canada.name: Canada
Canada.style: High-octane 4-4-2 'Maplepressing' — aggressive high press, forced turnovers and fast vertical transitions
keyPlayers: Alphonso Davies (Bayern Munich, LB, captain — back from 2025 ACL tear)
Canada.form: Seven-game unbeaten run in warm-ups; opened the World Cup with a 1-1 draw vs Bosnia (Lukic corner-kick header threatened, Larin super-sub cameo).
Canada.outlook: Elite athleticism, pressing and all three group games on home soil, but Davies (hamstring) misses the opener and Canada have never won a World Cup match (0-6).

Switzerland.name: Switzerland
Switzerland.style: Flexible, possession-oriented 4-2-3-1 flowing through Xhaka, with a miserly mid-block and fast breaks via Ndoye
keyPlayers: Granit Xhaka (Sunderland, CM, captain — record 145 caps, fourth World Cup)
Switzerland.form: Won UEFA qualifying group unbeaten (only 2 conceded); beat Jordan 4-1 and drew Australia 1-1 in warm-ups; opened the World Cup with a 1-1 draw vs Qatar — Embolo penalty 17', Khoukhi equalizer 90+4.
Switzerland.outlook: The group's best defense and most battle-tested spine, but conceding a last-gasp equalizer to Qatar raises questions about concentration — and the core is aging. They've stalled in second place at three straight World Cups.

Qatar.name: Qatar
Qatar.style: Spanish-school possession 4-3-3 with Afif free between the lines — open, entertaining and defensively leaky
keyPlayers: Akram Afif (Al Sadd, AM — 11 assists in qualifying)
Almoez Ali (Al Duhail, ST — AFC qualifying top scorer)
Qatar.form: Winless in six pre-tournament; opened the World Cup with a HISTORIC 1-1 draw vs Switzerland — Khoukhi's 90+4 equalizer earned Qatar their first-ever World Cup point in their 10th attempt.
Qatar.outlook: Qatar's resilience and Lopetegui's organisation earned a point no one foresaw; now Canada await at BC Place. The first point may unlock belief. Goals will still be hard to find, but a draw is no longer unthinkable.

Bosnia and Herzegovina.name: Bosnia and Herzegovina
Bosnia and Herzegovina.style: Compact 4-4-2, direct service into the Džeko–Demirović front two, with a heavy set-piece emphasis
keyPlayers: Edin Džeko (Schalke 04, ST, captain — 40 years old, record 72 international goals)
Bosnia and Herzegovina.form: Qualified via March playoff penalty wins over Wales and Italy; drew final warm-up 1-1 with Panama; opened the World Cup with a 1-1 draw vs Canada — Lukic's corner-kick header the standout moment, Džeko's shoulder kept him off the bench.
Bosnia and Herzegovina.outlook: Shootout-forged nerve was evident at BMO Field, and they're still level with Canada and Switzerland on 1 point. Džeko's shoulder fitness vs Switzerland is the swing factor between a famous qualification run and an early exit.

Brazil.name: Brazil
Brazil.style: Fluid 4-2-3-1/4-3-3 with an all-out front line — Vinícius left, Raphinha right — and rotating midfield shapes
keyPlayers: Vinícius Júnior (Real Madrid, FW — 22 goals in 53 club games in 2025-26)
Raphinha (Barcelona, W — 21 goals and 7 assists in 33 matches)
Neymar (Santos, FW — calf injury doubt, fourth World Cup)
Brazil.form: Won four of last six pre-tournament; drew 1-1 with Morocco in the opener at MetLife — conceded Saibari's strike in the 21st minute before Vinícius equalized in the 32nd, but couldn't find a winner.
Brazil.outlook: Elite wingers and five-star pedigree, but the leaky defence conceded to Morocco's debutant striker and Scotland now lead Group C. Brazil must chase group leadership through games vs Haiti and Scotland — the Neymar fitness saga shadows the camp.

Morocco.name: Morocco
Morocco.style: 4-2-3-1 morphing into 4-2-2-2 to open a motorway lane for Hakimi — more expansive and possession-dominant than the Regragui era
keyPlayers: Achraf Hakimi (PSG, RB, captain — reigning African Player of the Year)
Brahim Díaz (Real Madrid, AM — AFCON 2025 Golden Boot)
Morocco.form: Perfect 8-for-8 in CAF qualifying, AFCON 2025 champions; drew 1-1 with Brazil in the opener — Saibari opened the scoring in the 21st minute only for Vinícius to equalize in the 32nd. Controlled large stretches but couldn't hold.
Morocco.outlook: World-class spine and 2022 knockout pedigree, but the new manager's system is still finding its edge. A win vs Scotland would book their place in the knockouts and potentially the group crown.

Scotland.name: Scotland
Scotland.style: Organized 4-2-3-1 with set-piece strength and big-game midfield runners; can drop to a back five against elite sides
keyPlayers: Scott McTominay (Napoli, CM — 2024-25 Serie A MVP, scored the bicycle kick that sealed qualification)
John McGinn (Aston Villa, CM — scored the winner vs Haiti)
Scotland.form: First World Cup since 1998; warmed up with a 4-1 win over Curaçao and 4-0 over Bolivia; OPENED THE WORLD CUP WITH A WIN — McGinn's deflected strike in the 28th minute beat Haiti 1-0, putting Scotland TOP of Group C for the first time since 1974.
Scotland.outlook: Scotland are TOP of Group C on 3 points — their first World Cup win since 1998. Clarke's disciplined back-five absorbed Haiti's threat comfortably; Gilmour's absence and no elite striker remain the ceiling, but history is genuinely within reach if they draw Morocco.

Haiti.name: Haiti
Haiti.style: Compact, counter-attacking underdogs — young diaspora squad (average age 24) with genuine pace in transition
keyPlayers: Duckens Nazon (ST — all-time top scorer with 44 goals)
Haiti.form: Won their CONCACAF qualifying group despite playing every 'home' game abroad; beat New Zealand 4-0 then lost 2-1 to Peru; lost opener 0-1 to Scotland (McGinn 28') — Pierrot's late header flashed wide.
Haiti.outlook: The pilgrimage is still beautiful but the road narrows: Brazil await on matchday two, and elimination in the group stage now looks the likely ending. Isidor and Bellegarde carry the spirit forward regardless.

United States.name: United States
United States.style: Aggressive 4-2-3-1, high press and quick vertical transitions through the wide channels
United States.form: Shaky 2026: L 2-5 Belgium, L 0-2 Portugal, W 3-2 Senegal, L 1-2 Germany (Jun 6); but 2025 wins over Uruguay, Paraguay, Australia and Japan. Qualified as co-host.
United States.outlook: Opta gives 77% to qualify and 32.4% to win the group; home crowds at SoFi, Seattle and Santa Clara are the X-factor, but defensive lapses in 2026 friendlies are a real worry.

Turkey.name: Turkey
Turkey.style: Possession-based attacking 4-2-3-1 built around elite technical playmakers; can be open defensively
keyPlayers: Kenan Yıldız (Juventus, AM — RESTED vs Australia; expected to start vs Paraguay)
Turkey.form: Won UEFA Path C playoff; beat USA 2-1 in a 2025 friendly; golden generation peaking — but opened the World Cup with a 2-0 defeat to Australia with Yıldız rested, conceding in the 27th and 75th minutes.
Turkey.outlook: Under genuine elimination pressure after matchday one. Montella must deploy the full golden generation (Yıldız + Güler) vs Paraguay or risk a third consecutive World Cup group exit. The talent remains elite; the psychological shock is real.

Paraguay.name: Paraguay
Paraguay.style: Deep defensive block, physical 4-4-2, set-piece menace, transitions through Almirón
Paraguay.form: First World Cup since 2010; conceded only 10 goals in 18 CONMEBOL qualifiers; beat Argentina 2-1 at home — but were dismantled 1-4 by the USA press at SoFi (4 goals, an own goal). Their 'never concede twice in ten WC matches' record is shattered.
Paraguay.outlook: Opta: 64.3% was the pre-tournament read; reality is bleaker after conceding four. A horrible team to break down, but their defensive religion was exposed by rapid transitions. The Turkey game is now a must-win.

Australia.name: Australia
Australia.style: Disciplined 5-4-1 low block, compact mid-block, set-piece reliant — capable of ruthless transitions through Irankunda's pace
keyPlayers: Nestory Irankunda (19-year-old winger — youngest Australian WC goal scorer ever)
Australia.form: March friendlies incl. 5-1 win over Curaçao; opened the World Cup with a STUNNING 2-0 win over Turkey (Irankunda 27', Metcalfe 75') — just 28% possession, 1.32 xG conceded against 30 shots in the most resolute defensive performance of the tournament so far.
Australia.outlook: NOW TIED AT THE TOP OF GROUP D with the USA on 3 points after arguably the most shocking result of the group stage. The Socceroos' June 19 clash with the USA in Seattle is suddenly a battle for first place — not a survival game.

Germany.name: Germany
Germany.style: Flexible 4-2-3-1/3-4-2-1 positional play, aggressive counter-press, creativity funneled through Wirtz and Musiala
keyPlayers: Jamal Musiala (Bayern — recovered from broken leg, short of sharpness)
Germany.form: Won UEFA qualifying Group A; beat USA 2-1 on June 6; 4-3 win over Switzerland in March. Lost Lennart Karl to a thigh tear. OPENED with a 7-1 demolition of Curaçao — Nmecha, Havertz (×2), Schlotterbeck, Musiala, Undav and Brown all scored; Musiala's goal is the clearest signal yet that his post-injury sharpness has returned.
Germany.outlook: Joint Group E leaders with 3 points and the tournament's best MD1 goal difference (+6). Musiala's goal puts the fitness doubts to rest; the June 20 clash with Ivory Coast is now a first-vs-first battle for top spot.

Ecuador.name: Ecuador
Ecuador.style: Supremely organised, defence-first 4-2-3-1; conceded just 5 goals in 18 qualifiers but scored only 14 — wins ugly
keyPlayers: Enner Valencia (Pachuca, ST — all-time Ecuador World Cup scorer)
Ecuador.form: Lost only once since Beccacece took charge in 2024. CONMEBOL runner-up with a historic defensive record — but that 19-game unbeaten run ended 0-1 to Ivory Coast on matchday one; Ecuador hit the woodwork twice but Amad Diallo's 90th-minute volley sank them.
Ecuador.outlook: Under genuine elimination pressure at 0 points after MD1. Must beat Curaçao on June 20 and hope Germany beat Ivory Coast; the lack of goals is now the existential threat. Pacho and Hincapié intact, but Caicedo-starved creativity up front is a real problem.

Ivory Coast.name: Ivory Coast
Ivory Coast.style: Athletic 4-3-3, fast wide transitions, physical midfield; reigning AFCON pedigree
keyPlayers: Nicolas Pépé (Villarreal, RW — 8 goals, 10 assists this season)
Ivory Coast.form: Topped CAF Group F unbeaten (+25 goal difference) to reach a first World Cup since 2014. OPENED by ending Ecuador's 19-game unbeaten streak 1-0 — substitute Amad Diallo volleyed a 90th-minute winner after Ecuador hit the woodwork twice.
Ivory Coast.outlook: Joint Group E leaders with 3 points; the June 20 clash with Germany is a first-vs-first decider. Amad Diallo's late winner shows squad depth beyond Pépé and Adingra — the Elephants are a genuine last-16 outfit and no longer anyone's wildcard.

Curacao.name: Curacao
Curacao.style: Pragmatic Dutch-school 4-3-3 collapsing into a deep block; counters through Chong's dribbling
Curacao.form: Historic first World Cup — smallest nation ever to qualify — but lost all three friendlies since: 0-2 China, 1-5 Australia, 1-4 Scotland. Comenencia scored their first-ever World Cup goal to make it 1-1 against Germany in the 21st minute before the floodgates opened in a 1-7 defeat.
Curacao.outlook: Opta: 19% to qualify. Comenencia's strike gave the island one shining moment for the history books, but the 7 conceded in the opening match makes advancement near-impossible.

Netherlands.name: Netherlands
Netherlands.style: Possession 4-3-3 with Premier League-hardened spine; patient build through F. de Jong, width from Gakpo
keyPlayers: Memphis Depay (ST — all-time top scorer, 55 goals)
Netherlands.form: Won UEFA qualifying group; lost only one of their last 19 World Cup matches. Xavi Simons OUT (ACL); Frimpong also out. Dropped two points on matchday one — Kamada's 88th-minute equaliser earned Japan a 2-2 draw after the Dutch led, a lapse echoing Switzerland's 90+4 concession in Group B.
Netherlands.outlook: Opta: 48.2% to win the group, 88.2% to qualify. Their opener exposed concentration issues in the final minutes; Sweden's 5-1 form now makes them Group F's pace-setters, and the June 20 clash against the Swedes has become a genuine battle for first place rather than a coronation.

Japan.name: Japan
Japan.style: 3-4-2-1 with ferocious pressing triggers and rapid counters; proven giant-killers (beat Germany and Spain in 2022)
Japan.form: Scored 54 goals in AFC qualifying — most of any Asian side. But Kaoru Mitoma (hamstring) and Takumi Minamino (ACL) both miss the tournament. Eighth straight World Cup; never past the round of 16. Confirmed giant-killing credentials immediately — Kamada's 88th-minute equaliser against Netherlands earned a 2-2 draw and 1 point, once again proving Japan's press and nerve endure without their stars.
Japan.outlook: Opta: 76.2% to qualify. The Dutch draw suggests Japan can finish second; Kubo's form against Tunisia on June 20 could be the night the round of 16 ceiling finally shatters.

Sweden.name: Sweden
Sweden.style: Pragmatic, adaptable 4-2-3-1/back-five hybrid; defends in a mid-block and releases two elite strikers
keyPlayers: Viktor Gyökeres (Arsenal, ST — 19 league goals in Arsenal's title season)
Alexander Isak (Liverpool, ST — fitness concern, only 3 starts in 2026)
Sweden.form: Stormed the playoff path under Potter to reach a first World Cup since 2018. Kulusevski OUT injured. OPENED with the group stage's most emphatic win — 5-1 vs Tunisia in Monterrey: Isak scored (fitness doubts resolved), Gyökeres scored, and Yasin Ayari struck twice with stunning long-range efforts in a performance beyond any prediction.
Sweden.outlook: Group F leaders with 3 points and +4 goal difference. Isak and Gyökeres both fit and firing; the June 20 clash with Netherlands is now a battle for first place between the group's two expected qualifiers. Sweden are a genuine dark-horse contender well beyond the 62.6% qualifier odds.

Tunisia.name: Tunisia
Tunisia.style: Compact, disciplined low-to-mid block; defensively heroic but goal-shy, dangerous on set plays
Tunisia.form: Phenomenal qualifying — 9 wins in 10 and zero goals conceded — but upheaval since: Trabelsi sacked after AFCON; Lamouchi has managed only two games. The qualifying fortress crumbled immediately — hammered 1-5 by Sweden in Monterrey, conceding twice to Ayari long-rangers and once each to Isak, Gyökeres and Svanberg.
Tunisia.outlook: Opta: 43.4% to qualify — now near-impossible after a 1-5 MD1 loss. The defence does not travel; the goals never came. Japan on June 20 is a must-win but the momentum chasm is real.

Belgium.name: Belgium
Belgium.style: Possession-based 4-3-3 with rapid wide transitions through Doku and Trossard; De Bruyne as the No.10, Courtois anchoring a veteran spine.
keyPlayers: Jérémy Doku (Manchester City, LW — most dribbles completed in UEFA qualifying)
Belgium.form: Unbeaten through UEFA qualifying, then crushed Tunisia 5-0 in their final warm-up. OPENED the World Cup with a frustrating 1-1 draw vs Egypt in Seattle — Emam Ashour struck in the 19th minute and held Belgium for 47 minutes; Lukaku's 66th-minute introduction immediately caused a Mohamed Hany own goal, but Belgium's open-play creation was poor and De Bruyne's influence minimal throughout.
Belgium.outlook: Outlook darkened after the Egypt draw — Doku was neutralised, the forward line lacked cohesion without Lukaku from the start, and De Bruyne managed the game rather than controlled it. The June 21 duel with an Iran side that also dropped a point is now a must-not-lose. Group G is wide open.

Egypt.name: Egypt
Egypt.style: Compact, disciplined low-to-mid block built on the meanest defence in African qualifying, springing counters through Salah and Marmoush.
Egypt.form: Unbeaten qualifying campaign without conceding. OPENED the World Cup by leading Belgium 1-0 for 47 minutes — Emam Ashour struck in the 19th minute; Lukaku's 66th-minute introduction caused a Mohamed Hany own goal to level at 1-1. Egypt's defensive organisation exceeded all expectations; Salah was contained but a constant threat from the right.
Egypt.outlook: The MD1 defensive block confirms Hossam Hassan's structure is tournament-ready. Salah has yet to score but showed movement and quality throughout; Emam Ashour's opener proved the team can create without Salah dominating. New Zealand on June 21 is Egypt's clearest path to their first World Cup win in eight attempts.

Iran.name: Iran
Iran.style: Deep defensive block packed with veteran know-how, direct vertical service to Taremi; historically scores just 0.72 goals per World Cup match.
keyPlayers: Mehdi Taremi (Olympiacos, ST, captain — 59 goals in 103 caps)
Iran.form: Cruised through AFC qualifying to a fourth straight finals; preparations overshadowed by a difficult geopolitical backdrop. OPENED the World Cup with a shock 2-2 draw vs New Zealand — Ramin Rezaeian and Mohammad Mohebbi's late header rescued a point after Elijah Just's brace had put the All Whites 2-1 ahead. Iran's historically resolute defensive block failed to appear when it mattered most.
Iran.outlook: Under genuine pressure now: dropping a point against the lowest-ranked side in the tournament means Iran MUST beat Belgium on June 21 to stay alive. Taremi was largely anonymous and the defensive shape that won six Asian qualifying points failed against direct running. A crisis of confidence heading into SoFi.

New Zealand.name: New Zealand
New Zealand.style: Organised, physical 5-4-1, direct long balls and set pieces funnelled toward Chris Wood; competitive without the ball, limited creation with it.
keyPlayers: Chris Wood (Nottingham Forest, ST, captain — 45 goals in 88 caps)
New Zealand.form: Dominated OFC qualifying. AT THE WORLD CUP: Elijah Just's brace and Chris Wood's two assists earned a stunning 2-2 draw with Iran at SoFi — Just became the first All White to score twice in a World Cup match; Wood's hold-up play was exceptional, creating both goals by drawing defenders and feeding Just's runs.
New Zealand.outlook: The oracle's most wrong call so far. Wood fit and imposing; Just a clinical finisher; the transition counter-attack was crisp and well-drilled. New Zealand can score against anyone in this group — their June 21 date with Egypt in Vancouver is the group stage's most open fixture. A best-third passage to the knockouts is no longer a dream.

Spain.name: Spain
Spain.style: Positional-play 4-3-3, suffocating possession and high press; Euro 2024 champions on a 31-match competitive unbeaten run.
keyPlayers: Lamine Yamal (Barcelona, RW — nursing a hamstring, may be managed early)
Rodri (Manchester City, DM, captain, 2024 Ballon d'Or)
Spain.form: Qualifying: 5 wins 1 draw, 21 scored, 2 conceded. OPENED the World Cup with a shocking 0-0 draw vs Cape Verde in Atlanta — Lamine Yamal started on the bench (hamstring, came on 71st minute) and couldn't unlock the block; Gavi and Ferran Torres both ineffective; Spain had 27 shots but Vozinha's goalkeeping denied them all in the group stage's biggest upset so far.
Spain.outlook: Opta's tournament favourite faces a first crisis: 27 shots and zero goals is alarming. Yamal must start against Saudi Arabia on June 21 and Williams needs to rediscover his Athletic Club best. A comfortable group exit is no longer guaranteed, and the Guadalajara altitude duel with Uruguay now carries potential elimination weight.

Cape Verde.name: Cape Verde
Cape Verde.style: Aggressive, fearless 4-4-2 mid-block with nothing-to-lose counters; diaspora-built squad with no intention of parking the bus.
Cape Verde.form: Historic first qualification — won six of their final seven qualifiers. WORLD CUP DEBUT TRIUMPH: held European champions Spain to a 0-0 draw in Atlanta — Vozinha made save after save against 27 Spanish shots in the group stage's most stunning result to date. The Blue Sharks absorbed pressure for 90 minutes and never flinched.
Cape Verde.outlook: No longer just participants — Cape Verde are proven competitors at this level. Vozinha is a genuine tournament-quality goalkeeper. The June 21 test against Uruguay's Bielsa press is the acid test of their defensive organisation; a point or a win would put them in serious contention for second place or a best-third berth.

Saudi Arabia.name: Saudi Arabia
Saudi Arabia.manager: Georgios Donis (appointed April 2026 after Hervé Renard was sacked)
Saudi Arabia.style: Mid-tournament identity crisis: Renard's brave pressing game replaced by pragmatic, counter-based caution; 25 of 26 players from the domestic league.
keyPlayers: Salem Al-Dawsari (Al-Hilal, LW, captain — scorer of the 2022 winner vs Argentina)
Saudi Arabia.form: Coaching upheaval under Donis (appointed April 2026). OPENED the World Cup better than any prediction: drew 1-1 with Uruguay in Miami — Abdulelah Al-Amri opened the scoring in the first half; Maxi Araújo's equaliser denied Saudi a famous result. Al-Dawsari was a constant threat on the left throughout the 90 minutes.
Saudi Arabia.outlook: One point from MD1 reopens the Group H calculus. If Spain are vulnerable (as the 0-0 vs Cape Verde shows), Saudi's June 21 match with them becomes massive — a point there combined with beating Cape Verde could realistically send them through. The discipline under Donis was real and encouraging.

Uruguay.name: Uruguay
Uruguay.style: Bielsa's trademark man-to-man pressing and vertical chaos — a tournament-high 147 high turnovers in CONMEBOL qualifying; the post-Suárez/Cavani era.
keyPlayers: Federico Valverde (Real Madrid, CM — engine and leader)
Uruguay.form: Mixed friendlies, then a 5-1 loss to the USA. OPENED the World Cup with a frustrating 1-1 draw vs Saudi Arabia in Miami — Abdulelah Al-Amri's opening goal shocked Bielsa's press; Maxi Araújo rescued a point but Darwin Núñez failed to convert key chances and the high-turnover system that destroyed Italy in qualifying never clicked.
Uruguay.outlook: Far from convincing in MD1 — Núñez erratic, Valverde worked alone without support. Cape Verde on June 21 is a must-win: Bielsa's press against the same defensive discipline that held Spain to 27 shots poses the group's biggest tactical question. Uruguay's class is not in doubt; their ability to break a structured block is.

France.name: France
France.style: Pragmatic, transition-lethal 4-2-3-1; a tournament-hardened machine that has reached the final in four of the last seven World Cups. Mbappé central, Dembélé wide, Tchouaméni shielding.
keyPlayers: Kylian Mbappé (Real Madrid, ST, captain — 12 goals over the last two World Cups)
Ousmane Dembélé (PSG, FW, 2025 Ballon d'Or winner)
William Saliba (Arsenal, CB — playing through a back issue)
France.form: Qualified unbeaten, won four of last five, closed warm-ups with a 3-1 win over Northern Ireland. All 26 declared fit.
France.outlook: Opta: 95.3% to advance, 13% tournament winners (No.2 pick). Drawn into the closest thing to a Group of Death, but the ceiling remains the trophy.

Senegal.name: Senegal
Senegal.style: Athletic, physically imposing 4-3-3 with a granite spine and pace in wide areas; Africa's top-ranked side, though chance-creation can run dry against deep blocks.
keyPlayers: Sadio Mané (Al-Nassr, FW, talisman — returns at 34)
Senegal.form: Turbulent: won AFCON 2025 on the pitch only for the title to be stripped via a forfeit ruling — a wound and a motivation. Warm-ups: 2-3 loss to USA, 0-0 with Saudi Arabia; goals have dried up.
Senegal.outlook: Opta: 62% to advance. Third straight finals; the 2002 ghost (beating France in the opener) walks beside them, but Norway blocks the clean route to the last 16.

Iraq.name: Iraq
Iraq.style: Deep organised block, set-piece menace, channel balls to Aymen Hussein; resolute, limited, emotionally turbocharged at a first finals since 1986.
keyPlayers: Aymen Hussein (ST — 8 goals in AFC qualifying)
Iraq.form: Beat Bolivia in the March inter-confederation playoff to qualify, then lost their June warm-up to Venezuela; winless in all six previous World Cup matches (1986).
Iraq.outlook: Opta: 27.1% to advance — second-lowest of all 48 teams. The Lions of Mesopotamia's realistic dream is one historic point; the Senegal finale in Toronto is their best window.

Norway.name: Norway
Norway.style: Ruthless transition 4-4-2 — sit compact, then release Haaland, Sørloth and Nusa at terrifying speed; averaged 4.6 goals per qualifier, the highest by any European nation ever.
keyPlayers: Erling Haaland (Manchester City, ST — 16 qualifying goals)
Martin Ødegaard (Arsenal, AM, captain — fresh off Arsenal's first title in 22 years)
Norway.form: Perfect qualifying: 8 wins from 8, 37-5, including a 7-1 aggregate demolition of Italy. First World Cup since 1998 and the tournament's most feared dark horse.
Norway.outlook: Opta: 82.3% to advance, 25.2% to top the group. If Ødegaard stays fit to feed Haaland, Norway can genuinely take France's crown in Foxborough.

Argentina.name: Argentina
Argentina.style: Flexible 4-4-2/4-3-3, controlled possession with vertical bursts, world-class game management and a ferocious counter-press around Messi's free role
keyPlayers: Lionel Messi (Inter Miami, captain — still the fulcrum at 38)
Argentina.form: W 3-0 Iceland, W 2-0 Honduras (June friendlies); top of CONMEBOL qualifying; 17 of the Qatar 2022 winners retained.
Argentina.outlook: Overwhelming favourites: Opta gives 72% to top the group. Only heat, Messi's hamstring and complacency stand between them and the last 32.

Austria.name: Austria
Austria.style: Aggressive gegenpressing 4-2-2-2 — the lowest PPDA of all UEFA qualifiers, relentless transitions and structured set pieces
keyPlayers: David Alaba (Real Madrid, captain, CB — finally at a World Cup at 33)
Marcel Sabitzer (Dortmund, CM — led qualifying for chances created)
Christoph Baumgartner (RB Leipzig, AM — career-best 13 Bundesliga goals)
Marko Arnautović (Red Star Belgrade, ST — 8 qualifying goals)
Austria.form: W 1-0 Tunisia (June 1); topped qualifying group, first World Cup since 1998. Drilled to a Rangnick rhythm.
Austria.outlook: Opta's pick for second (67.4% to advance). The press travels; the question is whether ageing legs survive Texas heat.

Algeria.name: Algeria
Algeria.style: Patient 4-3-3 possession with devastating left-sided overloads; Petković's calm structure has tightened a once-chaotic defence
keyPlayers: Riyad Mahrez (Al-Ahli, captain, RW — 38 international goals)
Mohamed Amoura (Wolfsburg, FW — involved in 58% of Algeria's qualifying goals)
Algeria.form: Stunned the Netherlands 1-0 in Rotterdam (June 3); 8 qualifying wins, their best-ever campaign.
Algeria.outlook: Live dark horse at 57.1% (Opta) to advance. Amoura's pace against Austria's high line on the final matchday could decide second place.

Jordan.name: Jordan
Jordan.style: Compact 5-4-1 low-to-mid block, rapid counters through the right channel toward Al-Tamari; set-piece dependent
keyPlayers: Musa Al-Tamari (Rennes, W, captain — the 'Jordanian Messi')
Yazan Al-Naimat (FW — Asian Cup 2023 final hero)
Jordan.form: L 1-4 Switzerland, L 0-2 Colombia in June; Arab Cup 2025 runners-up; 32 qualifying goals — their best ever.
Jordan.outlook: Brave debutants (Opta 40.9%) but warm-up defeats exposed the gulf. The Algeria game in Santa Clara is their realistic point-harvest.

Portugal.name: Portugal
Portugal.style: 3-4-3/4-3-3 hybrid, technical midfield dominance through the PSG axis, patient possession with Ronaldo as penalty-box finisher
keyPlayers: Cristiano Ronaldo (Al-Nassr, captain, ST — 41, record caps, sixth World Cup)
Portugal.form: W 2-1 Nigeria (June 10), W 2-1 Chile (June 6); reigning Nations League champions, qualified top of their group.
Portugal.outlook: Opta: 59% to win the group, 94.9% to advance. The deepest squad in Group K; the only doubt is whether Ronaldo-centricity blunts them against elite pressing later — and two opening games in Houston heat.

Colombia.name: Colombia
Colombia.style: 4-2-3-1 with explosive wide play, Díaz cutting in from the left, James operating between lines; press-resistant and street-smart
keyPlayers: Luis Díaz (Bayern Munich, LW — one of the form wingers of 2025-26)
James Rodríguez (Minnesota United, AM — the eternal conductor)
Colombia.form: W 2-0 Jordan (June 8), W 3-1 Costa Rica (June 2); 28 qualifying goals, second only to Argentina; Copa América 2024 finalists.
Colombia.outlook: 84.9% to advance (Opta) and a fashionable pick to win the group. Comfortable at Azteca altitude; the Miami finale against Portugal likely decides top spot.

Uzbekistan.name: Uzbekistan
Uzbekistan.style: Disciplined 4-1-4-1 Italian-school block, compact central spine, counters funnelled to Shomurodov and Fayzullaev
keyPlayers: Abdukodir Khusanov (Manchester City, CB — short of minutes and sharpness)
Eldor Shomurodov (captain, ST — all-time top scorer)
Uzbekistan.form: L 1-2 Netherlands (June 8), L 0-2 Canada (June 2); superb qualifying record to reach a first-ever World Cup, but June friendlies exposed the level gap.
Uzbekistan.outlook: Opta: 22.1% to advance. Cannavaro's organisation gives them a puncher's chance, but Khusanov's rust and two warm-up defeats temper the debut dream.

DR Congo.name: DR Congo
DR Congo.style: Physical, well-drilled 4-3-3 mid-block; athletic transitions through Wissa and Bakambu; resilient knockout temperament forged in playoffs
keyPlayers: Axel Tuanzebe (DF — playoff hero vs Jamaica)
DR Congo.form: D 0-0 Denmark (June 3), L 1-2 Chile (June 9); won the intercontinental playoff to reach a first finals since Zaire 1974.
DR Congo.outlook: Opta: 22.1% to advance. Defensively credible — the Denmark draw proved it — but goals are scarce, and Houston's noon heat greets them first.

England.name: England
England.style: Pragmatic 4-2-3-1, German-engineered defensive structure, fast wide transitions through Saka and Gordon, Kane dropping to link
keyPlayers: Harry Kane (Bayern Munich, captain, ST — 36 Bundesliga goals in 31 games)
England.form: W 3-0 Costa Rica (June 10), W 1-0 New Zealand (June 6); perfect qualifying — 8 wins, 22 scored, 8 clean sheets. Foden and Palmer left home.
England.outlook: Opta: 67.5% to win the group, 11.2% to win the tournament (3rd favourite). Ruthless under Tuchel; the only flicker of doubt is Bellingham's rhythm and knockout scar tissue.

Croatia.name: Croatia
Croatia.style: 4-3-3 midfield-control football, Modrić advanced as a No.10 to spare his legs; slow build, set-piece danger, peerless tournament streetcraft
keyPlayers: Luka Modrić (AC Milan, captain, AM — 40, record sixth World Cup)
Joško Gvardiol (Manchester City, CB — just back from a fractured tibia)
Mateo Kovačić (Manchester City, CM — missed most of season with Achilles trouble)
Ivan Perišić (W — the eternal tournament performer)
Croatia.form: W 2-1 Slovenia (June 7), L 0-2 Belgium (June 2); unbeaten qualifying campaign. Fitness of Gvardiol and Kovačić is the great unknown.
Croatia.outlook: Opta: 76.9% to advance. Finalists 2018, third 2022 — but the golden midfield is 40, 36 and patched-up. Likely second, dangerous to anyone in a one-off knockout.

Ghana.name: Ghana
Ghana.manager: Carlos Queiroz (appointed after Otto Addo's March sacking)
Ghana.style: Emergency-rebuilt 4-4-2 low block, defensive caution with explosive breaks via Semenyo and Williams
keyPlayers: Antoine Semenyo (Manchester City, FW — 17 PL goals this season)
Jordan Ayew (Leicester City, captain, FW — 14 goal involvements in qualifying)
Ghana.form: D 1-1 Wales (June 2); 1 win in last 5. Mohammed Kudus OUT injured — he scored the goal that qualified them. No clean sheet in their last 10 World Cup games.
Ghana.outlook: Opta: 49.5% to advance, but chaos reigns: new coach, no Kudus, poor form. Semenyo's brilliance is the lottery ticket.

Panama.name: Panama
Panama.style: Energetic 4-4-2 pressing unit — led CONCACAF qualifying in high turnovers and pressed sequences; wing-back thrust through Murillo
keyPlayers: Adalberto Carrasquilla (Pumas UNAM, AM — the creative engine)
Amir Murillo (RB — 9 goals 12 assists in 90 caps)
Panama.form: D 1-1 Bosnia (June 6), L 2-6 Brazil (May 31), W 4-2 Dominican Republic; qualified unbeaten through both CONCACAF rounds.
Panama.outlook: Opta: 40% to advance — group outsiders, but organised, unbeaten in qualifying, and far better than the 2018 vintage. The Ghana opener is everything.

groups.groupReading: The high altars of Mexico anoint their hosts, who glide through on thin air and thick noise while Korea's swift spirits follow close behind. The Czech towers fall short of the top two yet may slip through the third-place gate; the southern lions roared bravely but depart with their discipline in tatters.

The glass's first vision shattered on matchday one: all four sides locked on one point apiece, Qatar having etched their name into history at the 90th minute's dying breath. The maple press and the Swiss wall remain the glass's favoured pair, but both must earn what they assumed was already written — Xhaka vs Lukic, David vs Afif's belief now alive. The final order may still read as foreseen, but only after the hardest second matchday anyone expected.

The glass's first night burned tartan: McGinn's deflected winner sent Scotland to the top of Group C — a position no Scots side has held at a World Cup since Archie Gemmill danced in Argentina. Yet the orb still foresees the giants recovering: Brazil's firepower overwhelms Haiti, Morocco's precision edge Scotland in Foxborough, and the June 24 collision in Miami under the lights remains the chapter that will be recited for decades. Scotland fall one place short of the top but walk through the third-place gate with their heads held high; Haiti's beautiful pilgrimage ends in song.

The glass cracked on its first two nights: the American press blew Paraguay apart at SoFi, then Australia's fortress-defence swallowed Turkey in Vancouver with just 28% possession — the Socceroos and the Stars and Stripes now share the summit. The June 19 collision in Seattle is the group's true decider, and the Turkish golden generation must win or face elimination at the hands of a Paraguay side they should outclass with Yıldız restored. The orb rewrites this circle daily.

The orb rewrote this group's order on day four: Germany's seven-goal thunder and Ivory Coast's last-gasp steal have both earned first place together, while Ecuador's 19-game unbeaten wall fell to a substitute's 90th-minute volley. The granite was always the problem — Beccacece's men must find a goal somewhere before the tournament ends their story. Germany and the Elephants march on; the Andes side must claw it back.

The orb did not foresee Monterrey's earthquake: Potter's Swedes buried Tunisia five-one with two Ayari thunderbolts and a first goal from a fit Alexander Isak, leaping to Group F's summit. The orange tide ebbed — Kamada's 88th-minute equaliser stole what Netherlands thought was won, and the second seat now belongs to whoever survives June 20's summit clash. Sweden's knives were always hidden; they are hidden no longer.

The orb shattered in SoFi when Elijah Just's double erased all rankings and left all four sides level on one point apiece — Belgium clung to a draw against Egypt's resolute wall, Iran's storied defensive block crumbled against Chris Wood's hold-up craft, and the crystal refuses to read a clear summit. De Bruyne must reclaim his throne; Salah must score at last; Taremi must channel Iran's historic resistance; Just must prove SoFi was no dream. The second matchday in Los Angeles and Vancouver rewrites everything.

The glass cracked in Atlanta and Miami on the fifth day: Spain's armada fired twenty-seven shots and Vozinha caught every one; in Miami the desert prince drew first blood and Bielsa's press could not find a second gear. All four sides stand on one point with the group entirely unwritten — the oracle sees Yamal healed and Spain restored, but Vozinha's gloves have stained the crystal's certainty and every second act in this group arrives with genuine consequence.

Here the glass darkens, for this is the death-group of the western draw: the world's elite, a Viking storm averaging four-and-a-half goals a game, and a wounded African champion stripped of its crown by men in suits. The runes say France by a whisker over Haaland's Norway, Senegal scrapping into the third-place lottery, and brave Iraq collecting memories.

The cards show a crown that does not slip: the champions of Lusail glide through, untroubled by desert winds or alpine presses. Behind them, the Austrian machine and the Algerian desert fox circle one another until the final night in Kansas City, where a single goal divides destiny.

Two crowned heads — one Iberian, one Andean — march in step toward the gilded gates, their final waltz in Miami merely deciding who leads the procession. Beneath them, the Leopard and the White Wolf claw for scraps of history, and the cards favour the Leopard's teeth over the Wolf's weary legs.

The Three Lions march behind a German drum, their qualifying ledger spotless — eight gates, eight clean sheets — and the cards see no force in this quartet to stain it. Behind them the old Croatian sorcerer conjures one last passage, while the Black Stars and the Canal-men wrestle in the dust for a third-place lifeline.

matches.reading: The orb glowed over the Azteca before a ball was kicked... thin air at 2,240 metres and eighty thousand voices were always going to smother a Bafana side that creates little against elite blocks. Quiñones' strike and Jiménez's header shimmer in the glass — though three red mists swirled that no seer could love.
matches.keyFactor: Altitude and Azteca crowd overwhelm South Africa

matches.reading: The mist parts to show Coufal's long throw thundering in and Krejčí rising — yet the spirits favor the silkier feet. Hwang In-beom's Feyenoord form burns brightest; Korea's transitions wear down a Czech back line short of pace before the night grows old, even as Son's chances flicker and fade.
matches.keyFactor: Korea's transition pace against a slow Czech back three

matches.reading: Schick's flame — nine goals in his last eight for Leverkusen — licks at a South African block stripped of the suspended Sithole and Zwane. The bones grant Mokoena one thunderbolt from a dead ball, but the tall ones from Prague score twice in Atlanta's sealed noon heat and end Bafana's road.
matches.keyFactor: South Africa's suspensions meet Schick's hot streak

matches.reading: Two winners circle each other in Guadalajara's night and the crystal clouds, for Montes' suspension leaves a door ajar for Son's diagonal runs. Yet Jiménez's gravity, Lozano's directness and a second home cauldron tilt the glass — a narrow El Tri verdict, though a draw whispers loudly from the edges.
matches.keyFactor: Home cauldron vs Mexico's makeshift, Montes-less defense

matches.reading: By the third turning of the cards Mexico may already be crowned, yet the Azteca never sleeps and Aguirre's bench runs deep. The Czech towers rattle the thin air once from a corner, but Vega and Mora run at tiring legs all evening and the hosts finish unbeaten on their mountain.
matches.keyFactor: Mexico's depth and a second Azteca night at altitude

matches.reading: In Monterrey's glass-walled furnace the orb shows taegeuk red... Korea need little here, but Son's farewell tour does not idle. Williams' gloves delay the inevitable and Mofokeng conjures one moment of joy, yet an attack that managed fifteen goals in ten qualifiers cannot answer twice.
matches.keyFactor: Korea's attacking class vs South Africa's blunt attack

matches.reading: The maple mist rises over BMO Field even without Davies, whose hamstring keeps the captain robed at kickoff. Marsch's press will harry a Bosnia built around a 40-year-old Džeko nursing a wounded shoulder — yet the old wolf bites once against the run of the cards before David, hungry after a lean Juventus year, settles a nervous home debut.
matches.keyFactor: Canada's press and home crowd vs Džeko's aging but cunning threat

matches.reading: The glass barely fogs here: a Swiss wall that conceded twice in all of qualifying meets a Qatar side winless in six and leaking goals. Ndoye's runs and Embolo's shoulders breach the maroon line either side of the hour; Afif conjures sparks in the Bay Area sun, but no fire.
matches.keyFactor: Switzerland's elite defense against winless, leaky Qatar

matches.reading: Under SoFi's silver roof the runes counsel caution — Xhaka must turn the wheel while Akanji's back line answers questions it never faced in qualifying. Switzerland allowed Khoukhi to equalise in the 94th minute against Qatar, a lapse of concentration that Bosnia's set-piece sorcery — Lukic's corner-kick header against Canada already confirmed the danger — will punish if repeated. Džeko, still nursing the shoulder that kept him on the bench in Toronto, remains the great unknown; if he starts, the aerial menace doubles. Yet Swiss technical superiority and Kobel's gloves are sterner than what a last-gasp Qatari defensive midfielder provided — two Nati strikes on either side of the break, with Bosnia landing one from a dead ball to keep the ledger honest.
matches.keyFactor: Switzerland's concentration after the Qatar lapse vs Bosnia's proven set-piece danger — Džeko shoulder fitness the decisive swing

matches.reading: Vancouver's harbour wind carries red waves — but the orb now reads a Qatar transformed by Khoukhi's last-gasp point against Switzerland. That 90+4 equalizer is not mere fortune; it is proof that Lopetegui's organisation can survive ninety minutes of pressure and bite at the death. Canada, still searching for their first group-stage win after the BMO draw, welcome Davies's return to tip the balance from the left — yet Afif and Almoez Ali will not merely ship goals politely in BC Place's smaller confines. Two Canadian strikes are the glass's verdict, but Qatar's belief earns one back before the final whistle.
matches.keyFactor: Canada's press and Davies's return vs Qatar's newly-found World Cup resolve — no longer a foregone conclusion

matches.reading: The scales tremble and refuse to settle — both keepers of this gate likely advance, and the spirits smell prudence in the air. Kobel's gloves against Davies' lightning, Xhaka's tempo against Marsch's storm... the orb shows a single strike apiece and handshakes at the whistle.
matches.keyFactor: Both sides likely already through; a draw suits everyone

matches.reading: Seattle's grey skies suit the dragons' last stand — and if the shoulder has healed enough to start, Džeko's header at the far post is all the prophecy Bosnia need. If not, Jovo Lukic proved at BMO Field that Bosnia do not need the old wolf to threaten set pieces; the same Bosnian 4-4-2 discipline that held Canada level can contain Afif's sparks long enough. Lopetegui's men will land one moment of craft, but Bosnia's shootout-forged nerve carries them through.
matches.keyFactor: Bosnia's set-piece threat (with or without Džeko starting) vs Qatar's soft defensive center

matches.reading: The orb churns gold and crimson over MetLife — Vinícius and Raphinha arrive in the form of their lives, yet Brazil have conceded in five straight and Militão's absence gapes at the back. Hakimi's motorway lane carves one answer and Bono stands tall, but the canary firepower edges a duel the bones call nearly even.
matches.keyFactor: Brazil's elite wingers vs their own leaky, depleted backline

matches.reading: Fifty-two years of waiting fills Gillette with Haitian song, yet the tartan spirits march colder and deeper into the night. McTominay's late surge and a set-piece thunderclap appear in the glass; Isidor's pace draws gasps from the mist, but no goals.
matches.keyFactor: Scotland's set pieces and superior squad depth

matches.reading: The Tartan Army return to Foxborough as group leaders — a sentence that has never been written before — but the glass cautions against the champagne. Clarke's back five, disciplined against Haiti's direct threat, now faces Hakimi's motorway lane and Brahim Díaz's left foot, a combination several grades above Nazon and Bellegarde. Without Gilmour's calm feet Scotland must surrender the ball for long ages; one moment of Atlas precision cuts through a Scottish organisation that cannot press for ninety minutes at this level. Morocco win narrowly, and the October-like Gillette night tells Scotland they must win Miami to make history.
matches.keyFactor: Morocco's attacking class — Hakimi and Brahim Díaz — vs Scotland's organised back five without Gilmour's ball progression

matches.reading: Philadelphia's cauldron shows no mercy in the glass: the widest talent gulf in Group C, and Ancelotti's men — already stung by Scotland's early lead in this group — smell a goal-difference feast against the tournament's thinnest squad. A draw against Morocco is not the start Brazil wanted; the response here must be overwhelming. Neymar, if the calf spirits permit, writes a cameo verse, and the canary firepower pours through every gap Haiti cannot close. Four canary strikes shimmer in the crystal, though Haiti's pace on the break conjures one long-distance threat that Cavaleiro's gloves smother.
matches.keyFactor: Brazil needing goals to chase Scotland and Morocco; Haiti's thinnest squad in the field can't resist elite pressing for 90 minutes

matches.reading: Miami's golden hour hosts a Group C decider the orb never foresaw: Scotland's McGinn-led warriors, already the first Scots to lead this group since 1974, against a Brazil side chasing a crown they dropped in Foxborough. If Clarke's men need only a draw and Brazil need a win, the tactical battle becomes existential for both. McTominay's late surge may forge one moment of tartan defiance under the Florida lights — but Vinícius and Raphinha in full canary cry, running at legs that have fought through two 90-minute battles, will find the seams in a back five that cannot contain this much individual quality for an entire game. One Scottish goal writes history; two Brazilian replies end the fairytale with honour intact.
matches.keyFactor: Brazil's attacking brilliance and Miami heat vs Scotland's back-five discipline — McTominay the one tartan card that can change the night

matches.reading: Atlanta's sealed roof traps the Atlas lions' roar — a side that won all eight qualifiers against the lowest-ranked dreamers in the field. Hakimi marauds down his motorway, Brahim finishes twice, and Bono's clean sheet is etched in the glass before the second candle burns down.
matches.keyFactor: Morocco's perfect-qualifying machine vs the tournament's thinnest squad

matches.reading: I see a cauldron of red, white and blue, and a guaraní wall that bends but does not easily break. Paraguay conceded but ten times in eighteen qualifiers, yet Pulisic's left-channel sorcery and the SoFi roar tilt the scales by a single goal. The bookmakers whisper USA -105 against +310 — the spirits agree, narrowly.
matches.keyFactor: Pulisic vs Paraguay's low block in front of a home crowd

matches.reading: An old curse follows the Socceroos — fallen in their opening match at five of six World Cups past. Under Vancouver's closed sky, Yıldız and Güler will find the gaps between Australia's three centre-backs, though Goodwin's set-piece deliveries conjure one moment of resistance.
matches.keyFactor: Turkey's elite creators against Australia's historically poor openers

matches.reading: Lumen Field's noise becomes a twelfth man for a Group D summit clash that no seer foresaw: two sides on three points, separated by a single goal of advantage for the hosts. Australia's fortress — 28% possession and just 1.32 xG conceded against Turkey's thirty shots — now faces a very different animal in Balogun's pressing relays and Reyna's trivela from another dimension. Pulisic, precautionary with a calf knock in the opener, is expected back to add the diagonal runs that Australia's back five will have to account for. Popovic's 5-4-1 will compress and absorb, as it did in Vancouver, while Irankunda hunts the same channel space that scored his historic opener; yet the American press is a deeper and more sustained threat than Montella's rotation squad. A single goal in Seattle settles a question no oracle expected to ask so soon.
matches.keyFactor: USA's press and home crowd vs Australia's resolute 5-4-1 fortress — the group's unexpected summit clash between two unbeaten sides

matches.reading: Under the Santa Clara night, two fallen titans face mutual annihilation — one licking wounds from a 2-0 humiliation at Australia's fortress, the other still reeling from four goals at SoFi. Yıldız, deliberately rested on matchday one, returns alongside Güler in Turkey's most complete attacking pairing; Paraguay's exposed back line — pace-thin and transitionally fragile, as SoFi proved — cannot also cope with width and the intricate link-up of two elite playmakers. The Guaraní will battle with honour and Sanabria will force one reply from a moment of counter-punch, but the golden crescent rises twice in the California dark to keep its last-16 dream alive.
matches.keyFactor: Yıldız returns after deliberate rest; Paraguay's defensive frailty against rapid transitions was exposed at SoFi — Turkey's creators exploit the same channels

matches.reading: SoFi's third act and the glass clouds with conditional fates. If Turkey recovered against Paraguay and the USA won in Seattle, both arrive through; Güler and Yıldız — rested on matchday one and reinvigorated by a survival win — will demand the stage they were always meant to own. The Americans, carrying a six-point group and a memory of the 2025 defeat in this exact matchup, will guard their lead but not surrender the night. An early Turkish flash — Güler has never met a big match he didn't adorn — is answered by an American equaliser, and prudence seals the draw for both sides who already hold their place.
matches.keyFactor: Turkey's golden generation (with Yıldız fully deployed) vs USA's structural depth — both teams likely through, a draw suits both

matches.reading: Australia are very likely through by Santa Clara's final curtain — the Socceroos need only guard what they've earned. Paraguay, their qualifying religion shattered by four SoFi goals, must win and hope the Turkey–USA result swings in their favour; but Popovic's fortress, which held thirty Turkish shots and kept 1.32 xG, is a different proposition from the American press that undid them. Almirón's spirit will carve one channel run and Goodwin's set-piece delivery conjures one Australian reply — parity on the night, and both sides exit with very different emotions.
matches.keyFactor: Australia's fortress defending vs Paraguay's need to score — and likely the last game of La Albirroja's 2026 campaign

matches.reading: The true final for second place arrives disguised as an opener. Ivorian lightning — Pépé and Adingra in full flight — crashes against a tricolor wall that surrendered five goals in eighteen qualifiers; Caicedo and Pacho absorb the storm, and each side leaves Philadelphia with a point and its destiny intact.
matches.keyFactor: Ecuador's elite defensive record vs Ivory Coast's transition pace

matches.reading: Under Houston's sealed roof, the orbs show a chasm of seventy-two ranking places. Curaçao shipped eleven goals across three warm-up defeats, and Wirtz's wand will conduct a procession; only Advocaat's old cunning keeps the score from true cruelty.
matches.keyFactor: Massive quality gap; Curaçao conceded 11 in their last 3 friendlies

matches.reading: A first-vs-first summit clash neither group predicted: the 7-1 machine against the side that stole three points with a 90th-minute volley. Musiala's goal against Curaçao proves the sharpness doubts are behind him — his interplay with Wirtz in Toronto's tighter confines is the duel Germany need to win and Ivory Coast need to survive. Amad's bench impact and Pépé's diagonal runs make this a genuine contest, but Kimmich's positional order and Neuer's experience tip the scales before the hour.
matches.keyFactor: Musiala back to full sharpness vs Ivory Coast's compact block and late-match impact from the bench

matches.reading: A must-win disguised as a formality — Beccacece's men arrive at Arrowhead with zero points and a goalscoring crisis that one match against the tournament's lowest-ranked side must resolve. Ecuador hit the woodwork twice against Ivory Coast but could not convert; Valencia, the World Cup's old executioner, is the only answer the cards can see. Caicedo's grip protects the middle while Curaçao's Comenencia — who scored against Germany's seven — provides the one moment of resistance before the Tricolor's superior class prevails.
matches.keyFactor: Ecuador's desperate need for goals and points vs a Curaçao side that shipped seven on matchday one

matches.reading: Needing goals and likely a victory to advance, the Elephants will stampede from the first whistle. Kessié's hundred caps of know-how and Adingra's pace overwhelm tired island legs in the Philadelphia sun; Chong conjures moments, but moments do not become goals against Ndicka.
matches.keyFactor: Ivory Coast needing a win and goal difference; Curaçao's fatigue and depth limits

matches.reading: The night the cards refused to read as kind: Ecuador, who needed this to be a mutual dead-rubber, instead arrive needing a miracle — zero points from MD1, a goalscoring crisis confirmed against Ivory Coast, and a Germany side seven-goals richer in confidence. Pacho and Hincapié's wall will hold longer than most, but Wirtz and Musiala in full concert at MetLife, likely needing only to guard what they hold, will find the seams. Ecuador's tournament ends in the east.
matches.keyFactor: Ecuador's goalscoring crisis vs a Germany side who opened with seven and Musiala in form

matches.reading: Beware, children of Oranje — the Samurai have slain greater empires in opening acts before. Yet the stars note Mitoma's absence dims Japan's left-flank blade, while Gakpo and Depay carry Liverpool-forged steel under Arlington's climate-controlled dome; the Dutch, beaten only once in their last nineteen World Cup matches, edge a genuine contest.
matches.keyFactor: Japan's giant-killing press blunted by Mitoma's and Minamino's absences

matches.reading: In Monterrey's heavy summer air, the unstoppable meets the immovable: Gyökeres, crowned in north London, against a Tunisian rampart that conceded nothing in ten qualifiers. Isak's uncertain legs and Potter's mere months of work leave Sweden short of fluency; Skhiri's shield holds until one Gyökeres thunderbolt is answered from a set play.
matches.keyFactor: Tunisia's zero-goals-conceded qualifying defence vs Sweden's unintegrated star power

matches.reading: A first-vs-second battle no oracle foresaw: Netherlands, who needed a last-minute rescue by Kamada to only draw, must now face a Sweden side that scored five. Houston's roof keeps the heat in but does nothing for Dutch concentration issues confirmed in minute 88 against Japan. Isak's fitness — confirmed against Tunisia — and Gyökeres' Bundesliga-forged ruthlessness give Sweden the finishing edge that the Dutch, without Simons and Frimpong, can no longer match in transition. Van Dijk's header may equalise, but Gakpo's chance-conversion under pressure is not what it was; the Blågult's momentum carries them through.
matches.keyFactor: Sweden's 5-1 confidence and two fit strikers vs Netherlands' late-game concentration issues and missing creators

matches.reading: The same Monterrey cauldron that witnessed Tunisia's five-goal humiliation now hosts Japan's next examination — and this time the opponent conceded nothing to Sweden's five. Tunisia's defensive fortress was always a myth the qualifying schedule created; Japan, who equalized Netherlands in the 88th minute on matchday one, arrive with the belief and the pressing triggers to expose it twice. Kubo's Basque-honed blade and the unit's collective press will carve this open before the hour; Tunisia must attack and cannot.
matches.keyFactor: Japan's collective press vs a Tunisia side whose defence gave up five goals and who must now chase the game

matches.reading: If Sweden beat Netherlands in Houston, this may only decide goal-difference margins; if Netherlands recover, Arlington could be a second-place decider. Sweden's Isak-Gyökeres axis is now the most dangerous front pair left in this group, and Japan, who can equalize but have not dominated any opponent for 90 minutes, face a different challenge from the Dutch pressing game or the Tunisian fortress. The Samurai's 88th-minute nerve is real — an early goal and an hour of collective discipline are entirely plausible — but Sweden's two-striker threat and squad momentum tip the final verdict to the Blågult.
matches.keyFactor: Sweden's Isak-Gyökeres front two and tournament momentum vs Japan's collective discipline and late-game nerve

matches.reading: Netherlands, stinging from their 2-2 concession in minute 88 against Japan and a likely first-place loss to Sweden, arrive in Kansas City needing a point and hungry for goals to improve goal difference. Tunisia, who already shipped five and have no path to qualification, must abandon their demolished fortress and go forward — and therein lies their doom twice over. Gakpo's hunting ground opens on both flanks; Depay, chasing his country's all-time scoring record with a tournament milestone in range, seals it. The Eagles of Carthage depart with one goal to their name from six attempts; the orange tide must recover.
matches.keyFactor: Netherlands needing a win for confidence and goal difference; Tunisia's fortress already broken and qualification gone

matches.reading: I see crimson waves crashing against a wall of granite that has not cracked in two years — yet Doku's lightning splits stone where Salah's counters only scorch it. The pharaoh strikes once on the break, but the old wizard of Napoli threads the dagger before the Seattle rain settles.
matches.keyFactor: Doku's dribbling vs an Egypt defence that conceded zero in qualifying

matches.reading: A nation playing with heavy hearts beneath hostile skies meets the tournament's humblest pilgrims, and grief becomes fuel. Taremi's single golden touch is all the prophecy requires, for Iran's matches are carved in 1-0 runes.
matches.keyFactor: Taremi's class against the lowest-ranked side in the tournament

matches.reading: The crystal is murkier than it was: Belgium's wide press could not open Egypt's wall and Iran's defensive fortress collapsed against New Zealand's direct running — both sides arrive at SoFi nursing wounds and needing points. Lukaku from the start tips the quality balance, and Doku in Inglewood's noon heat should find the space Iran's back line leaked against Just; but Iran, under pressure and playing with nothing to lose, will break forward where they were cautious with the All Whites. A second Belgian goal seals it, though Taremi may find a first touch of fortune.
matches.keyFactor: Lukaku starting from the first whistle vs an Iran side that must now attack after failing to hold New Zealand

matches.reading: Under Vancouver's closed roof two reinvented sides collide: New Zealand's Elijah Just and Chris Wood — who combined for both SoFi goals against Iran — demand Egypt's centre-backs engage in the duels the All Whites have perfected. Salah, goal-less in the tournament but smouldering, has the quality to find the seams in a New Zealand block that was never asked to defend in MD1 — Wood's side spent the entire Iran game on the attack. Egypt's superior squad depth and Salah's clinical threat give them the edge across ninety minutes, but Wood will test the pharaoh's defenders early and often. Egypt's first World Cup win comes in the final fifteen minutes — but this is no procession.
matches.keyFactor: New Zealand's Chris Wood and Elijah Just counter-attack vs Egypt's Salah-led class — this is the group's most open fixture

matches.reading: Two shields collide and the night grows quiet — this is a duel of locksmiths, not swordsmen, with second place and survival the wager. One moment of Salah sorcery near the death undoes ninety minutes of Persian resistance; Iran must then pray the third-place arithmetic is merciful.
matches.keyFactor: Two elite defensive blocks; one Salah moment decides it

matches.reading: A third-night showdown that will no longer be a dead rubber: New Zealand, who scored twice against Iran, will run Wood and Just at Belgium's back line without the restraint of underdogs. Belgium, whose open-play creation was poor against Egypt, can no longer coast — De Bruyne must perform or the Belgians' World Cup exit from Vancouver is a real scenario. Lukaku's physical presence and Doku's dribbling carry Belgium past Wood's well-organised counter, but the All Whites will not be swept aside.
matches.keyFactor: Belgium needing De Bruyne at his best vs New Zealand's counter-attack that proved it can score at this level

matches.reading: Under Atlanta's sealed roof the orb shows a thousand passes weaving a net around the brave islanders — courage is no antidote to Rodri's geometry. Pedri conducts, the Blue Sharks chase shadows, and even with Yamal wrapped in cotton wool the champions stroll.
matches.keyFactor: Spain's possession machine vs debutant nerves; climate-controlled venue suits tiki-taka

matches.reading: Miami's humidity tests Bielsa's running men, but a team that changed its general seven weeks before war cannot hide its confusion. Valverde seizes the second half by the throat; Núñez, chaos incarnate, adds the second. No Argentina-2022 lightning strikes twice here.
matches.keyFactor: Saudi coaching turmoil vs Bielsa's high turnovers

matches.reading: Back in the Atlanta sealed roof, and now it is Spain under existential pressure — Rodri's geometry and Pedri's control must unlock what twenty-seven shots could not against Cape Verde's Vozinha. Saudi Arabia drew 1-1 with Uruguay on MD1 and will not simply lie down: Donis's side showed shape and a cutting edge through Al-Dawsari. Yamal, expected to start this time and freed from the hamstring shackles, changes Spain's rhythm in the final third; two strikes find their way past a defence thinner than Vozinha's that was, but the three-goal procession the glass once showed has been washed away by the Cape Verde rain.
matches.keyFactor: Yamal starting for the first time vs a Saudi side with real shape under Donis — Spain need to win but the margin will be earned, not gifted

matches.reading: Bielsa's wolves must hunt against the same defensive wall that absorbed Spain's twenty-seven shots without conceding — and Uruguay know it. Valverde's long-range threat and Núñez's physical menace will ultimately be too much for Vozinha and the Blue Sharks to absorb for ninety minutes, and one moment of Araújo's aerial authority from a set piece breaks the spell; yet Cape Verde, now unbroken at this level, will find one counter-punch through Monteiro or Livramento before Uruguay seals it late. Two strikes for La Celeste, one for the island warriors who leave Miami with their heads held high.
matches.keyFactor: Uruguay's Bielsa press vs the defensive discipline that held Spain to 27 shots — Núñez must convert the chances his MD1 performance squandered

matches.reading: Houston's dome hosts the group's second most consequential night: Cape Verde, who held Spain to nothing, and Saudi Arabia, who took a point off Uruguay, have both shown they can compete. Vozinha's gloves against Al-Dawsari's left foot is the duel that defines it; Cape Verde's defensive organisation under pressure has been the tournament's most pleasant revelation, and the island belief — forged in ninety minutes against the European champions — carries them through a tight game by a single goal. The borrowed general from Athens cannot quite unlock what the boy-king of Madrid could not.
matches.keyFactor: Cape Verde's Vozinha and defensive solidity vs Saudi's Al-Dawsari — a tight, tense qualifier with one goal in it

matches.reading: At 1,500 metres where the air thins and truth arrives early, the match that decides Group H's order meets sides who have both been humbled: Spain's 27-shot zero and Uruguay's failure to beat Saudi Arabia have rewritten the ledger. If both sides arrive with qualification already secured this could be calm; if Spain need a win after the Saudi Arabia game or Uruguay need the points after Cape Verde, the altitude duel becomes a genuine battle. The orb sees Núñez finding his first goal of the tournament and Spain's bench depth equalising — Deschamps said the mountain group is where champions are forged; Deschamps is not here, but the lesson is.
matches.keyFactor: Guadalajara altitude and both sides' unresolved attacking crises — this game's stakes depend entirely on June 21's results

matches.venue: MetLife Stadium, East Rutherford (New York/New Jersey)
matches.reading: The spirits whisper of 2002, when Senegal toppled France in an opener — but this French side is no fading champion, and Senegal's well of goals has run dry. Mbappé, hunting the all-time record, strikes either side of Koulibaly's heroic resistance; the ghost stays buried.
matches.keyFactor: Senegal's blunt recent attack vs France's elite transitions

matches.reading: Forty years of waiting end for Iraq beneath a hail of Norwegian iron — sentiment is sweet, but Haaland does not deal in sentiment. The deep block holds for half an hour before Ødegaard's scalpel finds the seam; the giant feasts twice, as he did sixteen times on the road here.
matches.keyFactor: Haaland against the weakest defence in the group

matches.reading: The orb shows brotherly love withheld: Arnold's brave wall against the deepest squad on earth. Dembélé returns dancing, Doué and Olise queue behind him, and Iraq's set-piece prayers go unanswered — France rest legs in the final half hour with the job long done.
matches.keyFactor: France's overwhelming squad depth vs Iraq's winless World Cup history

matches.venue: MetLife Stadium, East Rutherford (New York/New Jersey)
matches.reading: Two storms meet over the Meadowlands — Senegal's wronged warriors, fighting for a stolen crown, against the north wind that buried Italy twice. Sarr and Mané land the first blow, but Sørloth rises at a corner and Haaland, inevitably, has the final word in a match of brutal beauty.
matches.keyFactor: Haaland-Sørloth aerial power vs Senegal's need to chase the game

matches.reading: The summit duel the fates have circled since December: the world's finest against the perfect-record raiders, with first place and a kinder bracket the prize. Haaland scores because he always scores; Mbappé answers because he always answers — and Deschamps, the old fox, will not lose a game he does not need to win.
matches.keyFactor: Both likely already qualified; group winner decided on goal difference

matches.reading: In Toronto the lions of Teranga must roar or go home, and a wounded lion is the most dangerous kind. Mané, in perhaps his last World Cup act, breaks Iraq's resistance before the hour; Ndiaye seals a victory that should be worth a best-third passage into the knockout light.
matches.keyFactor: Senegal's superior athleticism and desperation for goal difference

matches.reading: The spirits whisper of 2010's ghost — but this Argentina is no haunted side. Algeria's triumph in Rotterdam glows bright, yet the crystal shows Álvarez's press suffocating their build-up and Messi conjuring once under the Arrowhead lights. The fox runs bravely, but the lion eats first.
matches.keyFactor: Argentina's counter-press vs Algeria's short build-up

matches.reading: I see a red machine of pistons and pressing traps meeting a debutant still blinking in the World Cup glare. Jordan's warm-up wounds — four conceded to Switzerland, two to Colombia — foretell a back line overwhelmed by Baumgartner's late runs. Al-Tamari may flash like a comet, but comets do not stop machines.
matches.keyFactor: Rangnick's press against a back line that shipped 6 in two friendlies

matches.reading: A noon sun over Texas, though the great roof shields the worst of it. Austria's press will steal one moment of chaos — the orb shows Sabitzer striking — but pressing Messi's Argentina for ninety minutes is to chase smoke. The champions' extra class in both boxes tilts the scales late.
matches.keyFactor: Whether Austria can sustain an elite press against elite ball retention

matches.reading: Two Arab nations under a California dusk, and the cards favour the elder brother. Amoura's lightning will find the space behind Jordan's wing-backs that Switzerland found before him. Yet the debutants' pride is real — Al-Tamari etches his name on the scoresheet before the night belongs to Algeria.
matches.keyFactor: Amoura's pace in behind Jordan's adventurous wing-backs

matches.reading: The decisive rune: whoever wins likely takes second, and the orb clouds with tension. Austria's press meets Mahrez's guile in a duel of nerve, and I see weary Austrian legs after two high-octane outings conceding late possession.
